one_line_takeaway: A conceptual SoK that re-frames LLM adversarial attacks and defenses around four attacker objectives (privacy, integrity, availability, misuse) claimed as the first objective-driven taxonomy, useful as an orientation map but with no original experiments.
key_finding: Framing the LLM adversarial landscape by attacker objective (privacy/integrity/availability/misuse) rather than by technique yields cross-cutting insights that technique-based taxonomies miss: the same technique (e.g., backdoor or indirect prompt injection) recurs across multiple objectives, and mapping attacks to goals better aligns defenses with security priorities. The survey also observes that availability attacks (DoS/sponge) and verifiable privacy-preserving LLMs are comparatively underexplored, and that backdoor detection/unlearning for LLMs lags behind classical classification-model defenses. Note: all numeric attack/defense effectiveness figures in the paper are quoted from the surveyed primary works, not measured by the authors.
core_contribution: This is a survey / systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) paper, not an experimental one. Its own contribution is a novel objective-driven taxonomy of adversarial attacks on LLMs, organizing the threat landscape around four adversary GOALS rather than around attack techniques: (1) Privacy Breach (membership inference, model inversion, model extraction, PII recovery, prompt/system-prompt extraction), (2) Integrity Compromise (data poisoning, model editing, weight poisoning, contrastive-learning backdoors, adversarial examples, indirect prompt injection), (3) Availability Disruption (denial-of-service via sponge examples, indirect prompt injection, and RAG backdoors), and (4) Misuse (jailbreak, fraud, malware generation). It also supplies a technical scaffold: four vulnerable LLM components (data, prompts, weights, gradients) and a catalog of general techniques (data/weight poisoning, model editing, prompt injection, indirect prompt injection, backdoor, contrastive learning, adversarial examples, side channel), plus a survey of defenses (red teaming/fuzzing, optimization-based mitigations, response reformulation, randomized smoothing, differential privacy, alignment) and five proposed research directions. The claimed novelty is being the FIRST objective/motivation-centric taxonomy, contrasted against five prior survey taxonomies that are technique-, risk-, lifecycle-, modality-, or system-based.
threat_model: The paper does not define a single threat model; as a taxonomy it aggregates many. The unifying framing is the adversary's OBJECTIVE against a deployed LLM-based system (foundation model + fine-tuning + agent + optional RAG + guardrails). Adversary capabilities surveyed span black-box query-only access (membership inference, model extraction/leeching, prompt extraction, most jailbreaks, sponge query attacks), white-box/gradient access (gradient-leakage data reconstruction, GCG-style suffix optimization), and training/supply-chain access (data poisoning, weight poisoning, model-editing backdoors, RAG corpus poisoning). Attackers may be the end user (jailbreak, prompt injection), a third party planting content the model later ingests (indirect prompt injection, RAG backdoor), or a model/pipeline supplier (poisoned pre-trained/fine-tuned models). Assets at risk: training data and PII, proprietary system prompts, model IP/parameters, output integrity/trustworthiness, and service availability.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new empirical evidence, benchmark, or reproducible comparison; the taxonomy's superiority over prior taxonomies is argued qualitatively (Table 1 and prose) rather than validated. Coverage is a snapshot of pre-2025 literature (arXiv v1, Feb 2025) in a fast-moving field. The four objective categories overlap heavily (backdoors, indirect prompt injection, and poisoning appear under several objectives), so the taxonomy is not a clean partition. It is text-LLM-centric and does not systematically treat multimodal/agentic-tool-use attack surfaces. No quantitative meta-analysis, no severity/prevalence weighting, and defenses are described qualitatively without head-to-head effectiveness assessment by the authors.

quantitative_results: No original quantitative results: the paper reports no experiments of its own. The following numbers are all QUOTED FROM CITED PRIMARY WORKS, not measured by the authors.
(cited, Deng et al. [46]) multilingual prompts induced ChatGPT to output unsafe content at rates up to 80.92%.
(cited, Yong et al. [13]) translating objectionable English inputs into low-resource languages achieved harmful goals ~79% of the time.
(cited, SmoothLLM [64]) reduced GCG [42] jailbreak attack success rate to below 1%.
(cited, RA-LLM [68]) reduced attack success rate from nearly 100% to around 10% or less without retraining.
(cited, Tree of Attacks with Pruning [56]) jailbreak success rate up to 80% against SOTA LLMs including GPT-4.
(cited, GPTFuzzer [44]) reported over 90% mitigation/jailbreak-template success outperforming human-crafted templates.
(cited, DP-Forward [67]) outperformed DP-SGD by up to 7.7% utility at moderate privacy levels and ~3x more time/memory efficient.
(cited, PAIR [55]) often needs fewer than 20 queries to produce successful jailbreaks.
baselines_compared: The paper's own comparison is against five prior LLM adversarial-attack SURVEY taxonomies (Table 1), positioned as baselines to its objective-driven approach:
Risk-based taxonomy - Das et al. [15], 'Security and Privacy Challenges of LLMs: A Survey'
Lifecycle-based taxonomy - Wang et al. [16], 'Unique Security and Privacy Threats of LLM'
Modality-based taxonomy - Shayegani et al. [17], 'Survey of Vulnerabilities in LLMs Revealed by Adversarial Attacks'
Technique-based taxonomy - Chowdhury et al. [18], 'Breaking Down the Defenses: A Comparative Survey of Attacks on LLMs'
System-based taxonomy - Yao et al. [19], 'A survey on LLM security and privacy: The Good, The Bad, and The Ugly'
